input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a teleoperated 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its hardware consists of a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and dedicated autonomy elements that implement Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). The vehicle operates in an automotive ADAS context and serves as the observable physical element for the Digital Twin system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a UI lets operators and developers view, save, and generate reports of vehicle‑state data, display real‑time state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑set‑point calculation) runs on the DT (cloud) and returns the desired speed to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables remote enable/disable of ACC, setting of desired speed, and issuing of emergency‑braking commands. All services are delivered over a low‑latency, safety‑critical communication link.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a cloud‑based compute platform for offloading the ACC logic, a low‑latency communication stack between vehicle and DT, and a graphical user interface for monitoring and control. The software artefacts are hosted in the public GitHub repository https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin. The DT architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference model, employing the Observable Manufacturing Element concept, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each built from Functional Entities. The design may use OPC Unified Architecture for data exchange, as suggested by the ISO standard discussion.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The twin requires a shared notion of time between physical and digital components with clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms (the limit imposed by hardware). This 100 ms granularity is used for computation offloading and safety‑critical coordination; latency must be kept within this bound to maintain deterministic behavior.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system follows the ISO 23247 entity‑based hierarchy: a Physical Twin Entity, a Digital Twin Entity and a User Entity. Each entity is instantiated once and deployed on a distinct hardware component, forming a three‑tier hierarchy rather than multiple parallel twins. The User Interface FE is further split into separate visualization and control‑panel sub‑FEs. No decentralized or multiple twin instances are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture implemented as ROS 2 packages: each Functional Entity (FE) is packaged as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. Entities communicate via ROS 2 topics/services and share a common 100 ms synchronized clock. Resource allocation (compute, sensors, actuators) is mapped to the Physical Twin Entity, while data management and control reside in the Digital Twin Entity, and user interaction resides in the User Entity. Coordination is achieved through ROS 2 middleware and the shared time base.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration can be achieved by implementing Functional Entities using standards such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as suggested by ISO 23247. The architecture therefore allows data exchange with other enterprise systems or additional Digital Twins via OPC UA or other protocol adapters, though specific integration patterns are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture and maps software components to Functional Entities implemented as ROS 2 packages. The Digital Twin Entity hosts data‑management and control mechanisms, including a point‑cloud clustering and tracking function. A separate Data Storage component provides persistent data storage, though it does not map directly to a Functional Entity. All computational functionality is encapsulated in ROS 2 nodes that expose the models and data to the other entities.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The architecture requires a clock‑synchronisation accuracy of 100 ms to satisfy safety‑critical vehicular applications, reflecting a fidelity constraint on temporal data. No further validation, uncertainty quantification, or error‑correction procedures are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The physical twin streams raw sensor data—including LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering‑servo position, and vehicle‑state information—into the Physical Twin Entity’s Data Pre‑Processing Functional Entity (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking). This component clusters and tracks the raw point cloud (converting the raw points into a 2‑D representation and extracting object tracks) and forwards the pre‑processed streams to the Data Translation FE, which serialises the messages, aligns the message types, and performs unit conversions. The standardized data packets are then transmitted over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP (the inter‑entity transport layer) to the Digital Twin Entity, where they are received by ROS2 nodes on the remote side. The flow supports real‑time monitoring of ACC‑relevant data such as vehicle state, object tracks, and projected driving path.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the User Interface FE (Control Panel) – including ACC enable/disable, desired speed set‑points, and emergency‑brake commands – are packaged by the Data Translation FE into serialized UDP messages, sent over the Wi‑Fi link to the Physical Twin Entity’s Actuation FE, which forwards the commands to the motor and steering‑servo actuators. After actuation, the vehicle’s updated state (e.g., motor speed, steering angle) is reported back through the same data‑monitoring pipeline, providing implicit execution confirmation. The description does not specify explicit authentication or conflict‑resolution mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a hybrid network architecture: intra‑entity communication among software components is performed via ROS2 topics/services, while inter‑entity communication between the Physical Twin (on‑board vehicle hardware) and the Digital Twin (remote host) relies on a Wi‑Fi network employing UDP at the transport layer. The system requires low end‑to‑end latency to satisfy safety‑critical ACC operation, with a clock‑synchronisation accuracy of 100 ms as stipulated for vehicular applications. Bandwidth requirements are not quantified, but the use of UDP implies a lightweight protocol; reliability and redundancy mechanisms are not detailed in the documents, nor are specific security protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin services are implemented as ROS2 packages that can be deployed on remote computing resources such as cloud or edge servers; the case study demonstrates offloading the ACC algorithm to a cloud‑based DT. Computational requirements include sufficient CPU/GPU capacity to run lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑set‑point calculation, and 3D visualization, while persistent data (e.g., logs, reports) are stored by a Data Storage component. Scalability is implied by the modular ROS2 package architecture, but explicit scaling or redundancy strategies are not described. Deployment models support remote operation and management over the Wi‑Fi link, and the system is designed to maintain availability and reliability appropriate for safety‑critical applications, though concrete SLA figures are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Software components are first identified and related to sensors/actuators, then each component is mapped to a Functional Entity (FE). Every FE is implemented as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that provide the required functionality. The User Entity includes a User Interface FE split into a 3‑D visualization component and a control‑panel component. Data‑management functions are placed in the Digital Twin Entity, while data pre‑processing, actuation and generic automotive functions (e.g., point‑cloud clustering and tracking) reside in the Physical Twin Entity. Each entity is deployed on separate hardware, enabling distributed execution and clear ownership of functionality.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built on the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, adopting its entity‑based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and functional entities. The authors reference OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as an example protocol for device communication, in line with the standard. Additionally, the implementation respects vehicular safety standards that require clock synchronization accuracy finer than 100 ms, and the system targets a 100 ms synchronization precision.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.

them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
